title: Arrays
description: Store multiple items in a single, rigid container.

slides.title: Managing Groups 🗃️
slides.content:
## Too many variables!

Imagine you are building a game and need to store the top 5 high scores. Making 5 separate variables (`score1`, `score2`, etc.) is incredibly messy.

Instead, we can store them all together inside a single **Array**.

slides.title: Creating an Array 📦
slides.content:
To create an Array, use the `arrayOf()` function.

Items inside the array are ordered sequentially, starting from position `0` (the **Index**).

quizData.question: If you want to grab the very FIRST item out of an array, what index number must you use?
quizData.explanation: In programming, counting always starts at zero. Position 0 is the first slot in memory.

slides.title: Rigid Size 🧊
slides.content:
## Fixed Boundaries

Arrays have one major limitation: **Their size is permanently fixed.**

If you create an array with 4 items, it will ALWAYS have exactly 4 slots. You cannot simply `.add()` a 5th item to it later. It is frozen in size.

quizData.question: True or False: Once an Array is created with 10 slots, you can easily append an 11th item to the end of it.
quizData.explanation: Arrays allocate an exact, continuous block of memory. To change the size, you literally have to destroy the array and build a brand new one.
